results.title: Control Mode - tmux/tmux Wiki (official)
results.snippet: The authoritative reference for tmux -CC control mode: the text-only protocol George Nachman designed so a host app (iTerm2) can render tmux panes in its own native UI instead of via a raw terminal attach. Directly answers facet (4) on whether control mode is a better integration than raw attach for a programmatic Electron host, and explains the structured-output protocol you'd parse instead of a raw pty stream.

results.title: tmux Integration Best Practices - George Nachman / iterm2 Wiki
results.snippet: The single most relevant real-world precedent: the iTerm2 author's own best-practices for embedding tmux as an invisible backend so the host app provides all chrome. Covers control-mode pitfalls, window/tab tracking, and resize semantics that map almost exactly onto Grove's 'tmux as backend, app UI as only chrome' goal. Critical reading for facets (2), (3) and (4).

results.title: Getting Started - tmux/tmux Wiki (official)
results.snippet: Official wiki documenting new-session -A -s idempotent create-or-attach (the exact command Grove spawns), plus detach/attach lifecycle, window-size / aggressive-resize options, and how a window tracks attached-client size (smallest-client problem). Authoritative source for facets (1), (3) and (6).

results.title: iTerm2 + tmux -CC: The Remote Development Setup Nobody Talks About
results.snippet: 2024/2025 practitioner write-up of running tmux -CC as a transparent backend where the host (iTerm2) owns all tabs/windows and tmux survives disconnects and replays state on reattach. Concrete view of the UX-transparency payoff and tradeoffs (single-client, platform lock-in, reattach redraw) relevant to facets (2), (4) and (5).

results.title: Hide tmux status bar when zoomed in on a pane (GitHub gist)
results.snippet: Concrete config for making tmux's own chrome disappear: set -g status off plus an after-resize-pane hook to keep the status bar hidden. Directly supports the assigned angle (status off / single-pane / no extra chrome so the host app UI is the only chrome) for facet (2).

results.title: tmux control mode often fails to launch window when creating or attaching (iTerm2 issue)
results.snippet: Bug thread documenting a key gotcha: control mode intermittently fails to spawn a window when simultaneously creating-or-attaching a session (the new-session -A race), leaving the host stuck at 'tmux mode started'. Concrete pitfall evidence for combining facet (1) idempotent attach with facet (4) control mode in a scripted host.

results.title: Control Mode | tmux/tmux | DeepWiki
results.snippet: Directly addresses facet (4): tmux control mode (-CC) as a programmatic interface where external apps drive tmux over a text protocol via stdin/stdout, receiving structured event notifications instead of parsing raw screen output. This is the canonical reference for deciding control-mode vs raw attach for a programmatic host like Electron+node-pty, and pairs with the hooks/notifications model for tracking session lifecycle.

results.title: Control Mode — tmux/tmux Wiki
results.snippet: Authoritative upstream wiki on the -CC control-mode protocol: how panes map to host UI panes, the line-based notification stream, and the iTerm2-style renderer model. Most relevant primary source for evaluating control mode as the integration boundary (facet 4) and how attach replays state to a fresh client (facet 5).

results.title: Support for windows larger than visible on the attached client (tmux commit) — introduces window-size, default-size, resize-window
results.snippet: The commit that introduced the modern resize model directly behind facet (3): the window-size option (largest/smallest/manual), default-size, the resize-window command, and the legacy 'fixed to smallest client' behavior. Explains why a detached-then-reattached session may keep a stale size and how to make the tmux window track the host pane (set window-size, or resize-window) — the core gotcha when a single host pane drives one session.

results.title: Variable window sizes in tmux integration not working — both windows change size (iTerm2 issue)
results.snippet: Real-world bug report on the smallest-client / shared-window-size pitfall (facet 3): when multiple clients attach, tmux clamps the window to the smallest, so independently-sized host panes fight each other. Shows the exact failure mode and aggressive-resize/window-size workarounds a host must apply to keep each session sized to its own pane.

results.title: tmux Session Management: Complete Guide — Terminal Guide
results.snippet: Covers facets (1) and (6): the idempotent 'new-session -A -s' create-or-attach pattern vs explicit has-session guards, plus detach/attach/kill-session lifecycle and listing/cleaning sessions to avoid leaking orphaned detached sessions. Good practical baseline for the canonical backend spawn command.

results.title: tmux 3.6a: Scripted Sessions, Plugins, and Persistence — Botmonster Tech
results.snippet: Scripting-oriented (not interactive) take touching facets (1), (2), and (5): idempotent new -A -s for scripts, hiding chrome (status bar off, single pane), and capture-pane for recovering scrollback. Useful for the 'make tmux invisible so the host UI is the only chrome' requirement, though verify version-specific option names against the manpage.

results.title: Session and Window Lifecycle Commands | tmux/tmux DeepWiki
results.snippet: Directly addresses the detach-vs-kill-session lifecycle (angle 6). Documents that kill-session destroys one target session and detaches its clients but leaves windows linked elsewhere, vs kill-server tearing down everything. Critical for a scripted backend that must avoid leaking orphaned detached sessions and choose the right teardown verb on app exit/worktree removal.

results.title: Control Mode — tmux/tmux Wiki (official)
results.snippet: Authoritative reference for tmux -CC integration (angle 4). Explains the line-oriented % notification protocol (pane output, window/session changes), the -CC echo-disable behavior, and flow control where tmux pauses a pane that falls behind and the client resumes it. Directly informs whether control mode beats a raw attach for an Electron/node-pty host and what the redraw/replay tradeoffs are.

results.title: How to Efficiently Manage and Kill All Tmux Sessions (2026)
results.snippet: 2026-dated, directly on the orphaned-session leak problem (angle 6): warns that detaching produces 'ghost processes' and stale sessions that accumulate and eat memory, that each session holds a /tmp/tmux-UID socket, and shows scripted cleanup patterns (kill-session per name vs kill-server, and why kill-server is an ungraceful SIGKILL of in-flight jobs).

results.title: Capture tmux scrollback buffer (GitHub gist)
results.snippet: Concrete recipe for scrollback recovery (angle 5): capture-pane -S - to grab the full history then save-buffer to a file. Exactly the mechanism a host app needs to recover/persist scrollback that tmux does NOT dump to the outer terminal on detach — pair with a raised history-limit since the default is ~2000 lines.

results.title: How to use tmux control mode? — tmuxai.dev
results.snippet: Practical walk-through of -C/-CC control mode (angle 4): stdin/stdout command-and-notification protocol, % event types (pane output, window creation, session change) an external program uses to track tmux state, and flow-control pausing of lagging panes. Useful companion to the official wiki for implementing programmatic attach/redraw handling.

results.title: tmux(1) — terminal multiplexer manual page
results.snippet: Canonical reference covering the cross-cutting commands the question hinges on: new-session -A -s (create-or-attach idempotency, angle 1), capture-pane/history-limit (scrollback, angle 5), refresh-client/force redraw of an attached client (reattach replay, angle 3), and detach-client/kill-session semantics (angle 6). Single authoritative source to verify flag behavior when scripting tmux as a backend.

results.title: Control Mode — tmux/tmux Wiki (official protocol spec)
results.snippet: The canonical protocol reference for tmux -C/-CC. Documents the structured line protocol: command output blocks delimited by %begin/%end/%error, and async notifications (%output, %window-add, %layout-change, %window-size, %exit, %pane-mode-changed). Directly answers question (4): a programmatic host gets framed, parseable events instead of raw terminal bytes — but you must implement a stateful parser, octal-unescape %output payloads, and never confuse notifications with command-result blocks (notifications never appear inside an output block). Essential for deciding -CC vs raw attach.

results.title: tmux Integration — iTerm2 Documentation
results.snippet: iTerm2 is the reference implementation of a programmatic tmux host using -CC. Spells out the architectural tradeoffs a contrarian should weigh: the host UI becomes the only chrome (tmux status bar/keys disappear, panes render natively), but it inherits tmux's 'every client sees the same window' constraint — tabs can show empty/letterboxed areas because tmux sizes a window to the smallest attached client. Re-attach can lag while syncing large scrollback. Maps directly to questions (2) invisible tmux, (3) resize/fixed-to-smallest, (5) scrollback replay.

results.title: tmux -CC still determines window size after detaching · Issue #2594
results.snippet: Concrete control-mode pitfall: a -CC client continues to dictate window size even after detach, unlike non--CC sessions, leaving the window stuck at the old client's dimensions. This is exactly the question (3) reattach/resize gotcha and a strong data point for the contrarian case — control mode changes resize semantics in surprising ways versus raw attach (which honors aggressive-resize / window-size more predictably). Real maintainer discussion, not blogspam.

results.title: Implement tmux control mode (-CC) · Issue #5612 — microsoft/terminal
results.snippet: Windows Terminal's design discussion for adopting -CC. Valuable because it surfaces the cost side of the control-mode decision: the parser/state-machine complexity, scrollback handling, and pane-layout mirroring needed to host tmux structurally rather than just attaching raw. Good evidence for the 'is -CC worth it vs raw attach' framing, with engineers explicitly debating the integration burden.

results.title: Control Mode — tmux/tmux DeepWiki
results.snippet: Implementation-oriented walkthrough of how control mode is wired internally: commands run through the normal command queue with CMDQ_STATE_CONTROL set, %output escaping (chars < ASCII 32 and backslash become octal), and the guarantee that notifications never nest inside output blocks. Useful for actually writing a robust parser for a node-pty-backed host and understanding why raw byte handling differs from -CC framing.

results.title: tmux control mode fails to launch window when creating-or-attaching · iTerm2 #11174
results.snippet: Directly ties control mode to the create-or-attach idempotency question (1): when a -CC client both creates and attaches (the new-session -A pattern), the initial window/notification sequence can race and the window fails to launch. Concrete evidence that 'create-or-attach in one shot' interacts badly with control-mode's startup handshake — relevant pitfall for a host doing tmux new-session -A under -CC.

results.title: Advanced Use — tmux/tmux Wiki (official)
results.snippet: The authoritative reference for the window-size mechanics central to a programmatic host. Explains that a detached/new-session window draws its size from default-size (80x24 unless set with -x/-y), that window-size defaults to 'smallest' (window shrinks to the smallest attached client, padding larger clients with '·'), and the other modes largest/latest/manual. Crucially documents resize-window to force an exact size (which flips that window to window-size=manual) and aggressive-resize (constrain to clients on that window only, not the whole session). This is the core toolkit for making the tmux window track the host pane on attach/reattach.

results.title: tmux -CC still determines window size after detaching · Issue #2594
results.snippet: Directly on the reattach/detach sizing gotcha for a scripted host: after a control-mode (-CC) client detaches, the window retains the dimensions of that now-gone client instead of following the next/real client. The thread's workaround — explicitly issuing 'tmux resize-window -x W -y H' after attach — is exactly the pattern an Electron host should adopt to force the tmux window to match its pane rather than inheriting a stale size from a prior client.

results.title: Headlessly resizing tmux window/session · Issue #1367
results.snippet: Explains the 'fully-detached session keeps the creating terminal's size' behavior: a session created detached holds its default-size and does not pick up a new size until a client attaches, at which point it resizes immediately. Important for the app-restart/reattach flow — clarifies why the pane may briefly show the old geometry and that resize-window is the headless lever to set dimensions without an attached client.

results.title: session size in control master mode is 80x23 and cannot be changed to 80x24 · Issue #947
results.snippet: A concrete off-by-one / fixed-size pitfall when driving tmux in control-master mode: the session is stuck at 80x23 and resists resizing, illustrating how control mode and the smallest-client rule interact badly. Relevant for choosing between raw attach and -CC and for understanding why an embedding host must explicitly assert the desired rows/cols rather than trusting auto-sizing.

results.title: How to configure window size behavior in tmux?
results.snippet: A focused, current explainer of the four window-size modes (smallest default, largest, latest, manual) and how to set them via set-option -g window-size. Useful as a concise decision guide: an embedding host typically wants 'latest' or a manual resize-window per pane to avoid the smallest-client shrink-to-fit problem when multiple clients (or a leftover detached one) are connected.

results.title: variable window sizes in tmux integration not working — both windows change size
results.snippet: iTerm2's tmux -CC integration showing the smallest-client coupling in practice: resizing one window forces all windows to the same size unless aggressive-resize is on. Real-world evidence of the per-window vs per-session sizing trap that a control-mode-based Electron host would hit, and motivates aggressive-resize plus per-window resize-window to keep each pane independent.

claims.claim: tmux scrollback can be captured non-interactively from outside the session with a single CLI command: `tmux capture-pane -pS -10000 > buffer.txt`, where -p prints to stdout and -S -10000 starts 10,000 lines back in history.
claims.quote: tmux capture-pane -pS -10000 > buffer.txt

claims.claim: The -S flag sets the start line for capture; a negative value like -10000 reaches back into the scrollback history rather than just the visible pane.
claims.quote: `-S -10000`: Specifies the starting line position; `-10000` means capture from 10,000 lines back in history

claims.claim: Inside an interactive session the equivalent is the two-step `capture-pane -S -10000` followed by `save-buffer buffer.txt`, whereas the `-p` flag collapses this into one shot by printing to stdout instead of into a tmux paste buffer.
claims.quote: `-p`: Prints output to stdout instead of copying to a buffer

params.prompt: Extract key technical claims about tmux control mode (tmux -CC / control mode), especially: how control mode works for p

claims.claim: Running tmux with -CC (control mode) makes tmux send structured commands to the host terminal (iTerm2) instead of rendering its own text-mode interface, mapping each tmux window onto a native iTerm2 tab.
claims.quote: When you run tmux with `-CC` (control mode), instead of rendering a text-mode interface, tmux sends structured commands to iTerm2.

claims.claim: Under control mode, the host terminal provides native scrollback (trackpad scroll rather than Ctrl-b [) and native copy/paste, so the tmux text UI does not need to render its own chrome.
claims.quote: Native scrollback - scroll with your trackpad, not `Ctrl-b [`

claims.claim: The `-A` flag of `tmux new-session -A -s <name>` provides create-or-attach idempotency: the same command attaches if the session exists and creates it if not.
claims.quote: The `-A` flag is key. Run this command whether you're connecting fresh or reconnecting. If the session exists, you attach. If not, you create it.

claims.claim: tmux sessions persist on the server across disconnects, so reattaching restores every window/tab to its prior state with long-running processes still running.
claims.quote: Disconnect and reconnect - everything restores exactly

claims.claim: Control mode behaves poorly with multiple simultaneous clients: attaching from two machines at once causes problems.
claims.quote: One client at a time: If you attach from two Macs simultaneously, things get weird.

claims.claim: The tmux status bar can be hidden globally with the command `set -g status off` and re-enabled with `set -g status on`, which is the direct mechanism for removing tmux's own chrome so a host app's UI is the only visible chrome.
claims.quote: Automatically disabling the status bar during zoom using `set -g status off` ... Restoring visibility when exiting zoom with `set -g status on`

claims.claim: Pane zoom state can be detected programmatically by checking for the 'Z' flag in the pane format string, e.g. `tmux list-panes -F '#F' | grep -q Z`, which can then conditionally drive status-bar visibility.
claims.quote: bind-key z resize-pane -Z \; if-shell "tmux list-panes -F '#F' | grep -q Z" "set -g status off" "set -g status on"

claims.claim: A `set-hook -g after-resize-pane` hook can toggle the status bar after every pane resize (not just prefix-triggered zoom), allowing external/programmatic commands to drive status-bar visibility without manual keybindings.
claims.quote: The hook-based modification executes "after every resize instead of only after ones triggered by the Prefix + z combination," enabling external commands (like Vim plugins) to trigger automatic status bar hiding without manual keybinding invocation.

claims.claim: The per-client window-size mechanism added to resolve this (refresh-client -C@i:XxY) acts as a hard upper bound rather than a fixed manual size: the window will never be bigger than that size but can be smaller, which avoids conflicts when multiple clients of differing sizes attach.
claims.quote: Try this please which makes the per-client size set with `refresh-client -C@i:XxY` a hard limit, so the window will never be bigger than this (it can be smaller)

claims.claim: When a control-mode client and a regular (non-control) client are both attached, a non-control client smaller than the window shows only a clipped viewport into the larger window, and by default tmux uses the most-recently-used client's size, so typing in the non-control client resizes the window to its size.
claims.quote: Yes the non-control client can show only part of the window. By default it should use the size of the most recent, so if you type in the non-control client the window should become its size and the control mode client will be smaller.

claims.claim: In tmux control mode (control master mode), refresh-client -C is the mechanism a programmatic client uses to set window dimensions, e.g. `refresh-client -C 80,24` to request 80 columns by 24 rows.
claims.quote: refresh-client -C 80,24 does not change the window size; apparently tmux internally believes that the window is 80x24.

claims.claim: tmux control mode can leave the session at a default size (here 80x23) that the host must explicitly correct, and the default size of a control-mode window has historically been undocumented.
claims.quote: tmux from git at d149752 creates an 80x23 window, which breaks some of our tests that require the window to be the correct height.

claims.claim: There can be a mismatch between the size tmux internally believes the window is and the size actually rendered, so a refresh-client request to the believed size is silently ignored (a sizing-idempotency pitfall for scripted hosts).
claims.quote: It looks to me like there is some confusion within tmux about the window height, and this was previously hidden, because the client always reset the session window size.

claims.claim: Issuing a refresh-client -C to a different size and then back to the target reliably forces the resize, evidencing that a single refresh-client to the 'believed' size may not take effect.
claims.quote: stty -a consistently reported "23 rows; 80 columns" initially, and only after executing refresh-client -C 80,23 followed by another refresh-client -C 80,24 did the size change to "24 rows; 80 columns."

claims.claim: Behavior where the attaching client always reset the session window size was an earlier (now-changed) tmux behavior, indicating attach-time client-driven resizing semantics in tmux are version-dependent.
claims.quote: this was previously hidden, because the client always reset the session window size

claims.claim: tmux performs window/session resizing only when a client attaches, not on detach; there is no way to explicitly set a size on an unattached (detached) session.
claims.quote: Resize happens on attach, not on detach. There is no way to explicitly set a size on an unattached session.

claims.claim: A detached tmux session retains the size of the terminal that created it; resize-pane -x/-y, aggressive-resize, force-height/force-width, and main-pane-height/width all fail to change the size of a detached session.
claims.quote: if I go ahead and create a new session, detach from it, try and resize it, then dump the buffer, the session will still have the size of the terminal that created it.

claims.claim: Reattaching a client to a fully-detached tmux session triggers an immediate resize of that session to the attaching client's terminal size.
claims.quote: the moment I tmux -S tmux-socket a to reattach to the fully-detached session, it will immediately resize!

claims.claim: capture-pane combined with save-buffer can be used to dump a detached session's screen content programmatically (e.g. for read-only display), but the captured content reflects the stale create-time size, not a programmatically requested size.
claims.quote: I'd like to run a program headlessly within tmux, and periodically capture output for display in a webpage via capture-pane and save-buffer

claims.claim: As of this 2018 thread, the maintainer (nicm) acknowledged tmux had no mechanism to set the size of an unattached session, considering only future possibilities like making force-width/force-height set the size of unattached sessions.
claims.quote: You could just make force-width/force-height set the size of any unattached sessions immediately you don't need to pretend to attach.

options.label: Control mode -CC（正解但大工程）
options.description: 走 tmux -CC，Grove 自己 parse control 協定、把 pane bytes 餵回原生 xterm → scroll/search/selection 全部跟現在一樣，零退化。真正的 live-process 持久化。代價：全世界只有 iTerm2 做過，等於從頭實作協定 parser + pane/window 生命週期，scope 暴增、風險高。

options.label: 縮小範圍：resume + scrollback 還原
